name: HDMA (Human Development Multiomic Atlas)
description: Code, documentation, and data products from the Human Development Multiomic Atlas: paired single-cell chromatin accessibility (snATAC-seq) and gene expression (snRNA-seq) from 817,740 fetal cells across 12 organs, 203 cell types, and more than 1 million candidate cis-regulatory elements (cCREs). Includes ChromBPNet models trained per cell type to predict accessibility from local DNA sequence, and analyses identifying composite TF motif syntax that mediates cooperative transcription factor binding.

publication.title: Multiomics and deep learning dissect regulatory syntax in human development
publication.authors: Liu*, Jessa*, Kim*, Ng*, et al.

key_features: 817,740 fetal cells across 12 organs, 203 cell types
More than 1 million candidate cis-regulatory elements (cCREs) called from snATAC-seq
All data deposited on Zenodo (https://zenodo.org/communities/hdma); tracks viewable on WashU Genome Browser
relevance_to_atlas: HIGH. The atlas's noncoding-variant question is fundamentally about which fetal-brain regulatory elements matter and which TFs bind them. HDMA provides per-cell-type cCRE calls and ChromBPNet models for fetal tissue including brain, which is the developmental window most relevant to autism heritability (recall: 27/39 NDD genes have zero adult-brain eQTLs but several have prenatal-trending eQTLs in BrainVar). Direct application: lift Teagan-WGS noncoding variants over to HDMA cCREs and check whether any fall in cell-type-restricted fetal-brain cREs.
atlas_fit_questions: Do Teagan-WGS noncoding variants overlap HDMA fetal-brain cCREs at higher rate than expected by chance?
Among the autism-associated NDD genes with prenatal-trending eQTLs (MECP2, EP300), which HDMA fetal-brain cell types show their cCREs as accessible?
Does the HDMA composite-motif syntax explain why some autism GWAS loci show stronger effects than others (i.e., are the strong-effect loci ones that disrupt cooperative motif pairs)?
caveats: Fetal tissue, not autism cases vs controls -- this is a reference resource, not a case-control dataset.
ChromBPNet predictions trained per cell type but inference still requires the same caveats as ChromBPNet generally (predicts accessibility, not transcription).
Dataset is large (~1M cCREs) -- multiple-testing burden is real if running variant-enrichment tests.
needs_followup: Verify GitHub repo is the right one and that it's actively maintained
Pull the cCRE BED files from Zenodo and check overlap with current Teagan-WGS noncoding candidate list
Confirm whether the per-cell-type ChromBPNet models include any neuronal cell types relevant to autism
